content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 18, 2017

Amy Harrington
Sonoma City Council
752 Broadway
Sonoma, CA 95476

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-17-236

Dear Ms. Harrington:

      This letter responds to your request for advice regarding your duties as a Sonoma City
Councilmember under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

      Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090.

      Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) l
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

QUESTION

      May you, as an owner of residential property near the Sonoma Plaza Retail Overlay District
(the "District"), make, participate in making, or influence decisions involving a proposed temporary
moratorium prohibiting additional wine tasting rooms (and possibly Wine/Bars Taprooms) to be
located in the District?

CONCLUSION

      Based on the facts provided, it does not appear that it is reasonably foreseeable that the
moratorium would, based on the limited effects on traffic levels or intensity of use in your
neighborhood, influence the market value of your property.

___________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-236
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are a member of the Sonoma City Council and own your home in the City. The City
has a number of wine tasting facilities located around its Sonoma Plaza.² The City is contemplating
(i) adopting a temporary moratorium prohibiting additional wine tasting rooms to be located in the
Plaza Retail Overlay District, (ii) initiating studies to, among other things, determine the effects,
benefits and detriments of concentrating and permitting additional wine tasting rooms in this
District, and, (iii) perhaps, adopting policies and standards that would numerically limit and/or
otherwise regulate wine tasting facilities in this District.

You reside in and own a single-family residence located more than 500 feet from the
nearest, existing tasting room, and just over 500 feet from the nearest boundary of the Plaza Retail
District. Your property is not zoned commercial and cannot be used as a wine tasting facility. To
your knowledge, at this time, there are no other wine tasting facilities proposed to be located in the
Plaza Retail District.

According to the Supplemental Report for the Consideration of the Urgency Moratorium on
Wine Tasting Facilities (October 2, 2017, City Council meeting):³

"Moratorium Information: Under State law, an interim moratorium
ordinance may be adopted based on anticipated changes in development policies
arising from ongoing or anticipated planning studies, such as a zoning ordinance
amendment. At its Study Session of September 18, 2017 on Wine Tasting
Facilities, some background information regarding next steps and options was
provided to the City Council for consideration. While no formal direction was
asked for by staff or provided by the City Council, initial thoughts and
impressions were articulated by individual Council Members. At the conclusion
of the meeting, several Council Members expressed interest in exploring a
moratorium.

"The purpose of a moratorium is to "push the pause button" and allow a
public agency the time to study and review policy issues about a topic with the
current status/landscape not changing, while new regulations are considered. In
other instances, there are regional or state laws/regulations that are being
implemented that require time to review and unfold before a City can determine
its own course forward.

"An example of this is the City's recent cannabis moratoria. If the City
Council decides to adopt a moratorium, the following timelines are outlined in
State law. Upon adoption, the ordinance would go into effect immediately, but its
initial term is limited to 45 days, after which it would expire unless extended by a
vote of the City Council within that period. Under State law, the ordinance may

² According to the Sonoma Valley Visitors Bureau, "[t]he historic Sonoma Plaza lies at the heart of Sonoma
Valley— gateway region to Sonoma County. (http://www.sonomavalley.com/about/sonoma-plaza/)

³ We understand that no action was taken on that item in that meeting.

File No. A-17-236
Page No. 3

be extended twice: first for an additional period of ten months and 15 days and,
then, for one year, for a total of two years."

The City Council will also consider certain options with respect to the scope of the
moratorium:

1. Should they include Wine Tasting Facilities and Wine/Bars Taprooms in the moratorium.

2. Should they extend the area subject to the moratorium from Plaza Retail Overlay Zone to:

• The Downtown Planning Area
• The Historic Overlay Zone, or
• City-wide.

3. Adding possession of a signed lease for property to be used for wine tasting as an
additional basis for exemption from the moratorium.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest.⁴ A conflict of interest may arise only when it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will have a material financial effect on the official, the official's financial interests, or on the
official's immediate family that is distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. (Section
87103.)

The Act's conflict of interest provisions are generally analyzed with respect to foreseeability
and materiality on a decision-by-decision basis unless the decisions are "inextricably interrelated."
Decisions are inextricably interrelated when the result of one decision will effectively determine,
affirm, nullify, or alter the result of another decision. (Regulation 18706(b).) For example, where a
decision includes options or alternatives, these would all be considered a single decision for conflict
of interest purposes since selection of any option or alternative necessarily rejects the others.

Financial Interests

Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise are defined in Section 87103. Under
Sections 82033 and 87103(b), an official has an interest in any real property owned directly,
indirectly, or beneficially by the public official, or his or her immediate family, if the interest has a
fair market value of $2,000 or more. Your only interest implicated by the facts is your interest in
your personal residence.

⁴ A public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 (including city councilmembers) and who
has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting must: (1) publicly identify each financial
interest that gives rise to the disqualifying conflict of interest, (2) recuse himself or herself from the decision, and (3)
leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. (Section 87105; Regulation 18707.)

File No. A-17-236
Page No. 4

Foreseeability and Materiality

Regulation 1870|(a) provides that a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably
foreseeable if the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision
before the official or the official's agency. A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the
decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or
other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision
affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6).

Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6) generally covers decisions that directly affect an official's
property such as (1) the adoption of or amendment to a general, specific plan which includes your
property, (2) the zoning or rezoning of your property, (3) the imposition, repeal, or modification of
taxes, fees, or assessments on your property, (4) a decision to authorize the sale, purchase, or lease
of your property, (5) the issuance, denial or revocation of a license, permit or other land use
entitlement authorizing a specific use of or improvement to your property, (6) construction of, or
improvements to, streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, if your property will
receive new or improved services that are distinguishable from improvements and services that are
provided to or received by other similarly situated properties. None of these standards apply to the
decisions in question because your property is not the subject of the decision.

In all other cases, Regulation 18701(b) provides the standard of foreseeability. Under that
standard, a financial effect on an official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be
recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical; a financial effect need
not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable.

Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on
an interest will be material depending on the nature of the interest. Specific provisions implicated
by your facts are:

"(10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by
substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of
property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise
levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the
market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial
interest;

* * *

"(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision
was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the
market value of the official's property."

You provided the following facts:

• Your property will not be subject to the express terms of the Moratorium since your
property is not zoned commercial and cannot be used as a wine tasting facility.

File No. A-17-236
Page No. 5

• Your home is a little more than 500 feet from the nearest existing tasting room. The
decisions in question do not affect existing businesses.

• Your property is also a bit over 500 feet from the nearest boundary of the Plaza Retail
District. There are no other wine tasting facilities proposed to be located in the Plaza
Retail District.

Finally, in reviewing the property surrounding your property, we note that across the street is a
school/community center and on your street between your property and the Plaza appear to be
several businesses, residences and churches.

Thus, it does not appear that it is reasonably foreseeable that the moratorium for the Plaza
Retail District would, based on effects on traffic levels or intensity of use, influence the market
value of your property.

We also must consider the potential options to expand the moratorium either to the
Downtown Planning Area, the Historic Overlay Zone, or even city-wide. Generally, you may not
take part in decisions affecting property within 500 feet of your residence unless the Commission
determines there are sufficient facts to indicate there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable
impact on the property. (Regulation 18702.2(a)(11).) However, based on your facts, the wine
facilities in question are generally consolidated in the Plaza area and you are not within 500 feet of
any existing tasting room. Thus, we find no foreseeable measurable impact from the moratorium
unless the expansion would impact a proposed facility within 500 feet of your residence.⁵

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Jack C. Woodside
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division

JWW:jgl

⁵ If the expansion would impact a proposed facility in proximity to your home you may wish to request
additional advice based on those facts. As noted above, absent an exception, if one of the options will create a conflict
of interest you will be disqualified for the entire decision.

(the "District"), make, participate in making, or influence decisions involving a proposed temporary

located in the District?

neighborhood, influence the market value of your property.

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest.4 A conflict of interest may arise only when it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision 
will have a material financial effect on the official, the official's financial interests, or on the

87103.)

4 A public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 (including city councilmembers) and who 
has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting must: (I) publicly identify each financial 
interest that gives rise to the disqualifying conflict of interest, (2) recuse himself or herself from the decision, and (3) 
leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. (Section 87105; Regulation 18707.) 


Foreseeability and Materiality  
Regulation 1870l(a) provides that a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably 
foreseeable if the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision

other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision

Regulation 18702.2(a)(l)-(6) generally covers decisions that directly affect an official's

In all other cases, Regulation 1870 I (b) provides the standard of foreseeability. Under that

not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. 
Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on

(the "District"), make, participate in making, or influence decisions involving a proposed temporary 
moratorium prohibiting additional wine tasting rooms (and possibly Wine/Bars Taprooms) to be 
located in the District?

CONCLUSION: Based on the facts provided, it does not appear that it is reasonably foreseeable that the 
moratorium would, based on the limited effects on traffic levels or intensity of use in your 
neighborhood, influence the market value of your property.
